data.title: Build your own wealth transfer for retirement if you're not getting a big inheritance
data.description:
By Morey Stettner

So you don't have aging parents with hefty investment portfolios - what now?

Devising a strategy to attain your retirement goals - and following through - is within your control.

The "Great Wealth Transfer" is coming - it just might not be coming for you.

Over the next 20 years in the U.S., roughly $100 trillion is expected to flow from older generations to their heirs. This windfall - dubbed the Great Wealth Transfer - will give inheritors in their 30s, 40s and 50s opportunities to strengthen their retirement security.

Except most of us aren't in line for this handout.

"Folks who will get a large inheritance are not the norm," said Christopher Mallon, an adviser in Sturbridge, Mass. "About 80% of the general public will not get a great windfall from the Great Wealth Transfer."

So you don't have aging parents with hefty investment portfolios - what now?

The key, no matter what, is a commitment to prudent financial planning. "Rather than focus on a windfall that you won't receive, the question is, 'Can you achieve your financial goals?'" Mallon said.

Devising a strategy to attain your goals - and following through - is within your control. The fact that others may have more or less money, now or in the future, becomes irrelevant.

For example, people in their 30s who diligently contribute into their 401(k) and let it grow over decades will accumulate a sizable amount. By letting time and consistency work in their favor, they can secure a better retirement.

Besides, a share of the Great Wealth Transfer isn't a guarantee. "Retirees are living longer and spending more of their nest egg," Mallon said. "And they may have long-term-care needs" that erode their savings.

Anyone relying on an inheritance may find that the amount shrinks considerably given seniors' ever-lengthening life spans.

Like many advisers, Mallon urges his older clients to prioritize their own needs - and live large - rather than skimp and save so that their heirs inherit more. "Go on that vacation," he tells clients. "Don't deny yourself just to leave more money to your kids."

If you suspect that the Great Wealth Transfer will pass you by, there's always the possibility that you don't really know how much your parents actually have.

"Mom and dad might live in a small house and not be rich on paper," Mallon said. "But maybe there's a life-insurance policy the kids didn't know about. Or they might receive their parents' primary home or other noncash assets" that come as a surprise.

Some people who don't expect an inheritance might feel wistful or even sad about their financial prospects, especially when relatives and friends are on the receiving end.

Jesse Pantano, a chartered financial analyst in Miami, tries to shift those clients toward a more positive view. "I understand how you feel not expecting to get much of an inheritance," he might say. "Other clients have felt the same way. They found that by focusing on what they can control - their savings habits and spending patterns - and using the right levers to get on track, they achieve their retirement goals.

"This approach helps them get to a place of productivity - not just a feeling of being stuck," Pantano added.

More: Good news: We're living longer. Too bad most of us can't afford it.

Also read: 'How to actually do this successfully.' What are the best ways to negotiate fees with your financial adviser - but still get great service?

-Morey Stettner

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires

data.title: Best Rated Gold IRA Companies Announced - Top Precious Metals IRA Company Ranked for 2026
data.description:
As economic uncertainty, inflation concerns, and market volatility continue to shape the financial landscape, more investors are turning to Gold IRAs as a way to protect their retirement savings. IRAEmpire has published its best rated gold IRA companies 2026 rankings to help consumers make better-informed decisions.

BEVERLY HILLS, CALIFORNIA / ACCESS Newswire / February 14, 2026 / Physical gold and other precious metals have historically served as a hedge against inflation and currency devaluation, making them an attractive alternative to traditional paper-based investments.

Michael Hunt, Senior Writer at IRAEmpire says, "Opening a Gold IRA is not as simple as buying gold online. It requires working with a specialized company that can help you set up a self-directed IRA, purchase IRS-approved metals, and store them securely in an approved depository. Choosing the right provider is critical because fees, service quality, transparency, and expertise can vary dramatically from one company to another."

Overview of Best Rated Gold IRA Companies 2026:

  1. Augusta Precious Metals (Best for Large Investments)

  2. Lear Capital (Best Customer Support)

  3. Goldencrest Metals (Best Prices)

  4. Preserve Gold (Best Products)


He adds, "With dozens of Gold IRA companies competing for attention, many investors struggle to determine which providers are truly trustworthy and which ones should be avoided. That's why we've created this guide to the best rated Gold IRA companies for 2026."

Find the Best Gold IRA Company of Your US State

How IRAEmpire Ranked the Best Gold IRA Companies

Not all Gold IRA companies offer the same level of service, transparency, or value. To determine which providers truly deserve to be called the "best rated," IRAEmpire evaluated each company using a clear and consistent set of criteria.

Hunt shares, "Our rankings are based on real factors that matter most to retirement investors; not on advertising claims or promotional hype."

See the Full Best Gold IRA Companies 2026 List

Key Evaluation Criteria

Each Gold IRA company on this list was reviewed using the following standards:

1. Reputation and Customer Reviews

A company's track record is one of the strongest indicators of reliability.

IRAEmpire examined:

  • Better Business Bureau (BBB) ratings

  • Business Consumer Alliance (BCA) ratings

  • Trustpilot and third-party review platforms

  • Length of time in business

  • Overall customer satisfaction

Companies with consistent positive feedback and a long history of ethical business practices ranked highest.

2. Fees and Pricing Transparency

Gold IRA fees can significantly impact long-term returns. They prioritized companies that offer:

  • Clear and upfront pricing

  • Reasonable setup and annual fees

  • Transparent dealer markups

  • No hidden charges

Providers that openly explain their costs earned higher rankings than those with confusing or vague fee structures.

3. Product Selection and IRA Expertise

The best Gold IRA companies offer more than just a few coins. We looked for providers that offer:

  • A wide range of IRA-approved gold and silver products

  • Access to platinum and palladium options

  • Strong knowledge of IRS rules

  • Guidance on metal selection and portfolio diversification

Companies with deep expertise in precious metals IRAs scored better in this category.

4. Customer Support and Education

Investing in a Gold IRA can feel overwhelming, especially for first-time buyers. That's why we valued companies that provide:

  • Dedicated account representatives

  • Ongoing customer support

  • Educational resources and materials

  • Step-by-step rollover assistance

Providers that focus on investor education rather than high-pressure sales ranked at the top.

5. Rollover and Setup Process

A smooth IRA rollover process is essential. We evaluated how well each company handles:

  • Transfers from 401(k)s and IRAs

  • Paperwork and compliance

  • Coordination with custodians

  • Timeliness and ease of account setup

Companies that make the process simple and stress-free received higher marks.

6. Buyback Programs

A strong buyback policy is critical for investors who may want to liquidate metals in the future.

We reviewed whether each company offers:

  • Competitive buyback prices

  • Simple liquidation processes

  • Reliable customer service after the sale

Providers with fair and transparent buyback programs ranked higher.

Why These Criteria Matter

Choosing the wrong Gold IRA company can lead to:

  • Excessive fees

  • Poor customer service

  • Confusing processes

  • Limited investment options

By focusing on these evaluation factors, we identified companies that consistently deliver:

  • Trustworthy guidance

  • Fair pricing

  • Strong customer experiences

  • Long-term reliability

Top 4 Best Rated Gold IRA Companies (Official Rankings)

After reviewing dozens of precious metals providers using the criteria outlined above, four companies clearly stood out as the most reliable, transparent, and investor-friendly options for 2026.

These providers consistently deliver strong customer service, fair pricing, and expert guidance. This makes them the best choices for opening and managing a Gold IRA.

Each company on this list excels in different areas, but all four meet the highest standards for trustworthiness and professionalism.

#2 Lear Capital - Best for Flexible Investment Options

Coming in at #2 on our list of best rated Gold IRA companies is Lear Capital, one of the most well-known and established names in the precious metals industry.

Lear Capital has helped thousands of investors diversify their retirement savings with gold, silver, platinum, and palladium. The company earns its high ranking thanks to a broad product selection, competitive pricing, and strong IRA expertise.

Read Lear Capital Reviews and Analysis Here

For investors who want flexibility and variety in their precious metals portfolio, Lear Capital is an excellent choice.

Overview of Lear Capital

Founded in 1997, Lear Capital has decades of experience assisting investors with precious metals purchases and Gold IRA rollovers. Over the years, it has built a solid reputation for:

  • Industry knowledge

Lear Capital is particularly popular among investors who want more choices when building their precious metals portfolio.

Key Features of Lear Capital

Lear Capital stands out in several important areas:

Extensive Product Selection

  • Silver bullion

This makes it easier for investors to customize their retirement portfolio.

Real-Time Pricing Transparency

IRA Rollover Expertise

Investor Tools and Resources
Lear Capital offers educational materials, market insights, and personalized guidance to help both new and experienced investors.

#3 Goldencrest Metals - Best for Personalized Service

Ranked #3 among the best rated Gold IRA companies for 2026, Goldencrest Metals has quickly built a strong reputation for delivering highly personalized service and straightforward precious metals IRA solutions.

While some larger companies focus on volume, Goldencrest Metals distinguishes itself by emphasizing one-on-one guidance, transparent communication, and a customer-first approach. This makes it an excellent option for investors who want a more hands-on and supportive experience.

Read Goldencrest Metals Reviews and Analysis Here

What Makes Goldencrest Metals Stand Out

Goldencrest Metals has earned its place among the top providers by focusing on a few key strengths:

  • Personal attention for every client

  • Clear and honest pricing

  • Simple IRA rollover assistance

  • No-pressure sales environment

  • Growing base of satisfied customers

Rather than overwhelming investors with complex options, Goldencrest Metals focuses on helping clients make smart, comfortable decisions about protecting their retirement savings.

Key Benefits of Goldencrest Metals

Highly Personalized Support

One of Goldencrest Metals' biggest advantages is its consultative approach. Clients work directly with knowledgeable representatives who take time to understand their goals and risk tolerance.

Transparent and Competitive Fees

Goldencrest Metals is known for being upfront about pricing and costs, which helps investors avoid surprises later in the process.

Smooth and Simple IRA Setup

The company provides step-by-step assistance with:

  • Opening a self-directed IRA

  • Rolling over existing retirement accounts

  • Selecting IRS-approved metals

  • Coordinating secure storage

This level of guidance makes the process especially easy for first-time investors.

Strong Customer Satisfaction

Although newer than some competitors, Goldencrest Metals has quickly developed a loyal customer base and a growing number of positive reviews.

#4 Preserve Gold - Best for New Gold IRA Investors

Rounding out our list at #4 is Preserve Gold, a company that has built a strong reputation for making Gold IRA investing simple, accessible, and beginner-friendly.

While some precious metals providers cater primarily to experienced investors, Preserve Gold focuses on helping first-time buyers navigate the process with confidence. Its straightforward approach and helpful customer support make it an excellent entry point for those new to precious metals IRAs.

Read Preserve Gold Reviews and Analysis Here

Overview of Preserve Gold

Preserve Gold specializes in helping everyday investors protect their retirement savings with physical precious metals. The company emphasizes:

  • Easy account setup

  • Clear explanations

  • Step-by-step rollover assistance

  • Friendly, patient customer service

For investors who may feel intimidated by the idea of opening a Gold IRA, Preserve Gold offers a simple and supportive experience.

Key Advantages of Preserve Gold

Beginner-Friendly Process

Preserve Gold is known for breaking down complex IRA rules into easy-to-understand steps. This makes it ideal for people who are exploring precious metals for the first time.

Straightforward Onboarding

The company guides clients through:

  • Opening a self-directed IRA

  • Transferring funds from existing accounts

  • Selecting IRA-approved metals

  • Arranging secure storage

Everything is handled in a clear, organized manner with minimal confusion.

Helpful Educational Guidance

Preserve Gold provides practical information and personalized assistance to help investors understand:

  • How Gold IRAs work

  • IRS eligibility rules

  • Long-term benefits of precious metals

Responsive Customer Support

Clients often praise Preserve Gold for being patient, approachable, and willing to answer questions without pressure to buy.

Avoid Confusion By Finding the Top Rated Gold IRA Company of Your State

What Is a Gold IRA and How Does It Work?

Before choosing a Gold IRA company, it's important to clearly understand what a Gold IRA actually is and how it functions.

A Gold IRA (also known as a Precious Metals IRA) is a type of self-directed Individual Retirement Account that allows investors to hold physical gold and other IRS-approved precious metals instead of traditional assets like stocks, bonds, or mutual funds.

It provides the same tax advantages as a regular IRA-while giving investors the added security of owning tangible assets.

Access Your Free Gold IRA Guide Here

How a Gold IRA Differs From a Traditional IRA

In a standard IRA, your money is usually invested in paper assets such as:

  • Stocks

  • Bonds

  • ETFs

  • Mutual funds

A Gold IRA, on the other hand, allows you to invest in:

  • Physical gold

  • Silver

  • Platinum

  • Palladium

The key difference is that you own real, physical metals stored securely on your behalf, rather than financial instruments tied to the stock market.

Types of Gold IRAs

Just like traditional retirement accounts, Gold IRAs come in several forms:

  • Traditional Gold IRA - Contributions may be tax-deductible, and taxes are paid when you take distributions

  • Roth Gold IRA - Contributions are made with after-tax dollars, and qualified withdrawals are tax-free

  • SEP Gold IRA - Designed for self-employed individuals and small business owners

Each type offers unique tax advantages depending on your financial situation.

How a Gold IRA Works - The Basic Process

Opening and funding a Gold IRA typically follows these steps:

  1. Open a self-directed IRA with an approved custodian

  2. Fund the account through a rollover, transfer, or new contribution

  3. Choose IRA-approved precious metals

  4. Purchase metals through an authorized dealer

  5. Store the metals in an IRS-approved depository

At no point do you personally hold the gold-this ensures full compliance with IRS regulations.

IRS Rules and Compliance

Gold IRAs are heavily regulated to protect investors and maintain tax advantages.

Key rules include:

  • Only specific IRS-approved coins and bars are allowed

  • Metals must meet strict purity standards

  • Gold must be stored by an approved custodian

  • Home storage is not permitted

Working with a reputable Gold IRA company is essential to ensure all these requirements are properly followed.

Why Investors Choose Gold IRAs

Many people add gold to their retirement portfolio because it offers benefits that traditional assets often cannot provide, such as:

  • Protection against inflation

  • Hedge against market downturns

  • Portfolio diversification

  • Long-term wealth preservation

Unlike paper assets, physical gold has intrinsic value and has been trusted as a store of wealth for thousands of years.

How to Choose the Best Gold IRA Company

Selecting the right Gold IRA company is one of the most important decisions you'll make as an investor. The provider you choose will impact your costs, experience, and overall success with precious metals investing.

With so many companies to choose from, it helps to have a clear framework for evaluating your options.

Here are the most important factors to consider.

Find the Top-Rated Gold IRA Company of Your US State

1. Reputation and Trustworthiness

Always start by researching a company's reputation.

Look for:

  • High ratings with the Better Business Bureau (BBB)

  • Positive reviews on Trustpilot and other platforms

  • Long operating history

  • Few or no unresolved complaints

  • Strong industry credibility

A Gold IRA is a long-term investment, so you want a provider with a proven track record of honesty and reliability.

2. Fee Transparency

Fees can vary significantly between companies, and they directly affect your returns.

Before choosing a provider, make sure you understand:

  • Setup fees

  • Annual maintenance fees

  • Storage fees

  • Dealer markups

  • Any additional service charges

The best Gold IRA companies clearly explain all costs upfront. Avoid companies that are vague about pricing or reluctant to provide details.

3. Quality of Customer Support

Opening and managing a Gold IRA can be complex, especially for first-time investors.

Choose a company that offers:

  • Dedicated account representatives

  • Patient, knowledgeable guidance

  • Ongoing customer service

  • No-pressure sales approach

Top-rated companies like Augusta Precious Metals and Goldencrest Metals are known for their strong, investor-friendly support.

4. Product Selection

A good Gold IRA provider should offer a wide range of IRS-approved metals, including:

  • Gold coins and bars

  • Silver options

  • Platinum and palladium choices

Having multiple options allows you to diversify your portfolio according to your goals.

5. Buyback Program

Eventually, you may want to sell your metals. A strong buyback policy makes this process simple.

Look for companies that provide:

  • Competitive buyback prices

  • Fast and easy liquidation

  • Transparent resale terms

A reliable buyback program is an important feature for long-term investors.

6. Experience With IRA Rollovers

Most Gold IRA investors are rolling over funds from an existing 401(k) or IRA.

Choose a provider that:

  • Specializes in rollovers and transfers

  • Handles paperwork on your behalf

  • Coordinates smoothly with custodians

  • Ensures IRS compliance

Companies with deep rollover experience can save you time, stress, and potential tax issues.

7. Storage and Custodian Options

Your metals must be stored in an IRS-approved depository through a qualified custodian.

Make sure the company offers:

  • Secure, insured storage facilities

  • Trusted custodial partners

  • Clear explanations of storage fees

  • Flexible storage options

The best providers have established relationships with reputable custodians and depositories.

Red Flags to Avoid

Be cautious of any Gold IRA company that:

  • Uses high-pressure sales tactics

  • Makes unrealistic profit promises

  • Hides fees or refuses to disclose costs

  • Pushes collectible coins instead of IRA-approved bullion

  • Has poor online reviews or unresolved complaints

These are warning signs that a company may not have your best interests in mind.

Compare the Best Gold IRA Companies of USA 2026 Here

Gold IRA Fees Explained

One of the most important aspects of choosing a Gold IRA company is understanding the costs involved. Unlike traditional IRAs that hold paper assets, a precious metals IRA includes additional services such as physical storage and custodial management-which means extra fees.

Knowing exactly what you will pay helps you avoid surprises and choose a provider that offers the best overall value.

Learn More in this Free Gold IRA Fees Guide

Common Types of Gold IRA Fees

Most Gold IRAs involve several standard fees. While the exact amounts vary by company, these are the typical costs you can expect:

1. Account Setup Fees

This is a one-time fee charged when you first open your Gold IRA.

It usually covers:

  • Creating the self-directed IRA

  • Processing paperwork

  • Establishing custodial services

Setup fees generally range from $50 to $200, depending on the provider.

2. Annual Maintenance Fees

Custodians charge ongoing annual fees to manage and administer your account.

These fees cover:

  • Record keeping

  • IRS reporting

  • Account servicing

  • Compliance management

Annual maintenance fees typically range from $75 to $300 per year.

3. Storage Fees

Because IRA gold must be stored in an approved depository, investors pay yearly storage costs.

Storage fees depend on:

  • The total value of metals

  • Type of storage (segregated vs non-segregated)

  • The specific depository used

Most storage fees fall between $100 and $300 per year.

Segregated storage-where your metals are kept separate from others-often costs slightly more.

4. Dealer Markups and Premiums

When you purchase precious metals for your IRA, you don't pay only the spot price of gold. Dealers charge a premium to cover:

  • Minting costs

  • Distribution

  • Company profit margins

These markups vary based on:

  • Type of coin or bar

  • Market demand

  • Company pricing policies

This is why comparing dealers is so important-premiums can differ widely.

5. Transaction and Shipping Fees

Some providers charge small additional fees for:

  • Buying or selling metals

  • Shipping metals to the depository

  • Processing special transactions

Top-rated companies are usually transparent about these costs upfront.

Read The Full Free Guide on Gold IRA Fees

About IRAEmpire.com

IRAEmpire.com provides unbiased research, rankings, and educational resources to help Americans make informed decisions about Gold IRAs, precious metals, and retirement planning. Our mission is to offer transparent, data-driven guidance so investors can confidently protect and diversify their wealth with trusted gold investment companies across the United States.

CONTACT:
Ryan Paulson
ryan@iraempire.com

data.title: I'm almost 65 and live abroad. Should I skip signing up for Medicare?
data.description:
By Beth Pinsker

Is it worth it to pay a monthly fee for coverage you might never use?

Medicare does not cover you abroad, in most cases.

Got a question about investing, how it fits into your overall financial plan and what strategies can help you make the most out of your money? You can write to me at beth.pinsker@marketwatch.com. Please put "Fix My Portfolio" in the subject line.

Dear Fix My Portfolio,

I'm a longtime U.S. expatriate. While I've spent most of my adult life working overseas, I paid the max into Social Security and my 401(k) (which is now an IRA). As I approach retirement, I'm not sure what I should do about Medicare. I'm married to a foreign national, and we intend to retire where she's from in a few years. I'll be too old to enter the public healthcare system there; that means private insurance, which is expensive and may have low limits. I'm wondering, in the event I need major, critical or long-term treatment for a serious illness or condition, whether I qualify for Medicare back in the U.S.? At what age? What should I do to ensure I'd be able to tap it if I need it? And when should I do it?

Lost Abroad

Dear Lost,

It's good that you're asking about this ahead of time, because you need to make some moves now with Medicare so you don't end up paying more down the road. This is not just about deciding when to sign up. Medicare is a complicated program and it takes almost daily interaction for some.

"Once you enroll in Medicare, you're not done with it. It's easy to make mistakes," said Kimberly Lankford, author of a new guide, "Medicare 101," in a recent interview with MarketWatch.

If there's any financial topic where you need to keep some sort of handy guide on the shelf to answer questions, Medicare is that topic. You'll consult it over and over.

In the case of an expat who is approaching 65, you have to pay really close attention to the sign-up deadlines. You'll likely want to sign up for Part A right away - your window is the three months before the month you turn 65 to the third month after your birthday. If you have paid into the system for your adult working life, this will be free, so it's a no-brainer to sign up when you are eligible. If you don't, however, you may face penalties later on when you try to sign up.

If you aren't planning on living in the U.S. or visiting often, you may never use that benefit. Medicare doesn't cover you for emergency hospital care overseas, except in certain extenuating circumstances where a foreign hospital may be closer than a U.S. one. If you get sick wherever you are living, you'll have to have insurance that covers you for local care out of pocket.

Part B decisions

It's going to be harder to figure out the best plan for the rest of your medical care. Medicare Part B covers regular doctor visits and tests, and requires a monthly premium. You'd also need either a Medigap supplemental plan and Part D drug plan, or a Medicare Advantage plan. If you aren't going to be using that care ever, it might not be worth it for you.

If you think you're going to want to come back to the U.S. at some point, when your medical needs are more critical, you can't just start up Part B whenever you'd like. If you enroll after your window is closed, you'll end up paying a lifetime premium for a delayed start.

As Lankford notes in "Medicare 101," that's 10% on top of the base monthly premium set each year by the government, for each 12 months that you're late. The increase stays on your permanent record. For 2026, the monthly Part B cost is $202.90. If you, for instance, were five years late signing up because you were overseas, then you'd be paying more like $305 per month. If you end up with income-related surcharges (IRMAA), your bills could be huge.

So basically, your choices are to pay all along or to pay a greater amount when you do finally sign up. This is going to be a personal choice that you make once you run the numbers for yourself and determine how much time you'd actually be spending in the U.S. or how long it would be before you came back. For this, you also want to consider your family history. If there's a real chance that you'll be coming back for extensive medical treatment because cancer runs in your family, paying for Part B might make sense.

Keep in mind, always, that everything with Medicare pricing is subject to change. "Every year, Part B changes, and a lot of the numbers change," said Lankford. "But strategies stay the same."

Do you have questions about taxes? Join Don't Short Yourself Live to ask MarketWatch's Andrew Keshner and Beth Pinsker your tax questions on Wednesday, Feb. 25, at 1:30 p.m. Eastern time.

You can also join the Retirement conversation in our Facebook community: Retire Better with MarketWatch.

data.title: My husband, 73, wants to sell our $300K rental and buy an annuity. Is that wise?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'He believes the annuity will net $1,600 per month, the same as the rent from the house'

"He spent $40,000 of his savings on remodeling. The house is now finished. It would sell for $300,000." (Photo subjects are models.)

Dear Quentin,

My husband, 73, just remodeled his rental house. It is in beautiful shape and he is considering selling it rather than continuing to use it as a rental.

He spent $40,000 of his savings on remodeling. The house is now finished. It would sell for $300,000. Alternatively, the rent would bring in $1,600 per month. He plans to put $200,000 from the sale into an annuity. He believes the annuity will net $1,600 per month, the same as the rent from the house, and would also pay me for life if I became a widow. (I am 70.)

What would be the wisest option?

The Wife

Don't miss: I'm 65 and retiring next year. Should I buy an annuity to protect against diminished Social Security?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

If you invested $300,000 in the S&P 500, with a 7% annual return, you would have $374,000 after 20 years - even if you withdrew $1,600 a month.

Dear Wife,

I am not sold on your annuity plan.

While it may provide a guaranteed $1,600 monthly income if you chose a joint and survivor annuity, it ties that money up. You may need a large sum of money as you get older, especially if you or your husband needs long-term care. Without insurance, that can cost as much as $10,000 a month. This liquidity risk is real, especially because immediate annuities are typically irreversible and provide little or no access to the principal.

Maintaining the status quo gives you exactly the same income ($1,600 a month), but you have the option to sell the house at a later date, likely at a higher price if the value continues to rise. That said, the $1,600 monthly rent is a gross figure; net income after vacancies, repairs, insurance, property taxes and capital expenditures may be meaningfully lower. Yes, you would still have to be landlords, and that involves investments of time and money, but you could also think about hiring a management company to oversee the rental.

If you sold the house and netted $200,000 after capital-gains tax, and you invested that money in the S&P 500, with a 7% annual return, your funds would not quite make it to 20 years - if you withdrew $1,600 a month. If you had an average 10% annual return, you would end up with $1.3 million after 20 years. That is, you would receive your income - and you'd still have your capital.

Of course, these projections assume steady average returns and do not account for sequence-of-returns risk, the risk that market downturns occur early in retirement, or just before. A $1,600 monthly withdrawal from $200,000 represents nearly a 10% annual withdrawal rate, well above what is generally considered sustainable for retirees.

Immediate annuities

A little background on annuities: Immediate annuities provide a guaranteed income, similar to Social Security. The income depends on your initial investment, the interest rate you lock in and the payment schedule (monthly, quarterly or annually). A basic annuity with a 5% interest rate would give you $10,000 a year if you invested $200,000. In practice, payouts depend heavily on age, gender, and whether the annuity is joint-and-survivor.

If you are in very good health and have reasonably good genes, it can be a good bet. But by putting $200,000 of your investable wealth into an annuity, you are locking up that cash. Put another way, you have less liquidity. You are foregoing any increases in the stock market over the term of the annuity - but also hedging your bets against a severe downturn in the market.

For a couple like you in your early 70s, there's a (happy) chance that one of you will live a very long time - possibly into your 90s. That creates the risk of running out of money. A joint-and-survivor annuity helps protect against this by providing a steady income for as long as either spouse is alive, so the surviving spouse won't be left without income if the other one dies.

An annuity is neither a bond nor cash. It is an insurance contract. An immediate annuity gives you income immediately, as its name suggests, whereas you may have to wait 12 months with other annuity contracts. You are already protected from inflation with your 401(k)s and Roth IRAs, and with Social Security. Annuity income is typically only partially inflation-protected unless an inflation rider is purchased, which reduces initial payouts. You can read more about immediate annuities here.

Being 'conservative'

In your early 70s, you should be "moderately conservative" with your assets, says Charles Schwab. That means investing 40% in stocks, 50% in bonds and 10% in cash or cash equivalents. At the age of 80 and above, you should be "conservative," with 20% in stocks, but that also depends on your other sources of income (Social Security, 401(k)s, IRAs etc.) and your risk tolerance. These allocations, however, make sustaining a 9%-10% withdrawal rate from a $200,000 portfolio highly unlikely without depleting principal.

"At the start of every year, make sure you have enough cash on hand to supplement your regular annual income from annuities, pensions, Social Security, rental, and other regular income," the financial-services company adds. "Hold the money in a relatively safe, liquid account, such as an interest-bearing bank account or money-market fund."

Locking up such a large sum of cash now seems like as risky a move as investing it in the stock market or continuing to rely on your rental income, even though the guaranteed monthly income sounds attractive. A more balanced approach - annuitizing part of your proceeds, delaying annuitization to a later age and/or combining investment assets with guaranteed income - may be worth exploring.

Talk to a fiduciary about your options, including tax considerations, and make sure you're not being sold or oversold products by a commission-based adviser. You both appear to have done well managing your own assets up until now.

Don't go against your instincts.

Related: 'I got seriously burned': My financial adviser took me for lunch, bought my kids gifts - and had me invest $500,000 in annuities. What should I do?

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'I changed my will to deter vultures': I'm in my 60s and disabled. Do I spend down my assets to qualify for Medicaid?

'My retirement is going to be a disaster': I'm 59 and have $45,000 in my 401(k). I earn $72,000. Am I doomed?

I have early Alzheimer's and my husband has stage 4 kidney disease. We just inherited $50K. How can this help us?

Check out The Moneyist's private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

  (END) Dow Jones Newswires

data.title: Thank 'Too Late' Jerome Powell for the jobs and inflation beats
data.description:
By Brett Arends

Will the president's Federal Reserve nominee Kevin Warsh be as tough?

America wins because the Fed chairman refused to kowtow to the Oval Office.

The big news from the economy this week is that inflation is coming down, the jobs market is better than expected and real wages are rising.

So Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell and his colleagues are getting credit for a job well done, right?

Not a chance. The president was on TV this week with his former chief economic adviser, and both of them were badmouthing Powell and his stewardship of the Fed. The administration has launched a criminal investigation into the chairman, and is also trying to fire the deputy chair using the old Soviet Union trick generally known as Beria's law.

This is a potential worry for retirees - and everyone else who owns bonds or who relies on their investments for income. Inflation is terrible for retirees; falling inflation is good. Inflation is falling right now only because Powell has ignored the gibberish and insults from the Oval Office for a year and stuck to his guns.

The question now is: Will Kevin Warsh, the president's pick to be the next Fed chairman, show the same independence and guts?

We have no idea. Wall Street is hopeful: The market reaction since Warsh was announced as the nominee has been positive. But if Warsh really is going to steer a path completely independent of the president, you have to ask how he got the nomination.

This is not a president who tolerates any disloyalty or dissent (just ask Marjorie Taylor Greene). As he explained on TV this week, the president really, really wants the Fed to slash short-term interest rates - at least by half, and possibly to match Switzerland at 0%.

At this juncture, it is useful to look at where we are and where we go next.

Make no mistake: Falling inflation and strong jobs, if those numbers persist, are Powell's achievements. He was put under unprecedented pressure to slash short-term rates prematurely. The president publicly labeled him "too late," a "loser" and more, and insisted nearly all last year that short-term rates should be 1% or even lower. (The president also called the Fed chairman a "numbskull.") The vice president, meanwhile, said Powell was guilty of "monetary malpractice." And the president's attorney general launched a criminal investigation into him.

Where would the economy be if the Fed had buckled to the political pressure? We can only imagine. But you would walk a long way to find a single economist anywhere who thinks inflation would be coming down if interest rates had been slashed last year - especially if, as the president wanted, they were slashed to 1% or lower.

And this is your regular reminder to remember the context when the media and politicians talk about the Fed and "interest rates." There are lots of interest rates in the economy; the one that the president wanted Powell to cut was the overnight fed-funds rate.

Probably more important for the entire economy are other interest rates that the Fed does not control directly, and may only influence indirectly. These include the critical interest rate, or yield, on the 10-year Treasury note BX:TMUBMUSD10Y, plus the extra interest on top of that that corporations have to pay to issue their own bonds.

And when you look there, you'll see that Jerome Powell and the Fed have cut rates - big time.

The yield on the 10-year Treasury - 4.6% when the president took office last year, and 4.47% as recently as last summer - tumbled again Friday morning to below 4.1%.

Meanwhile, U.S. companies can borrow money more cheaply than they could when the president was inaugurated last year. That means they can get easier access to money to invest and boost the economy. The average yield charged on bonds has fallen both for investment-grade companies and for riskier high-yield bonds.

And that's despite a big jump in their interest rates last spring, when the president's ill-fated "liberation day" fiasco plunged markets into turmoil.

All of this is good news for the economy, and especially for those who live on fixed incomes.

If inflation continues to fall, next year's Social Security cost-of-living adjustment, or COLA, will be less than this year's. That's good news - actually, it's excellent news. The higher the COLA, the worse it is for seniors, because it means inflation is higher. And the COLA comes a year in arrears.

Meanwhile, the only longer-term Treasury bonds I own are inflation-protected TIPS. I underperform the bond market on big "up" days - but I sleep at night.

-Brett Arends

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  02-14-26 1305ET

data.title: The actual age limit for buying life insurance might surprise you
data.description:
By Morey Stettner

Life insurance in your 50s and 60s can make sense - but expect to pay up even if you're in good health

Term insurance can bridge the gap until you retire.

Life-insurance proceeds can help pay estate taxes.

When you're raising a family, life insurance matters. If you're the breadwinner and you die, you want to protect your spouse and young kids from financial hardship.

But once your kids reach adulthood and are independent, you may figure there's no longer any need for life insurance. That's especially true if you have little or no debt and enough savings to last a few years even in a worst-case scenario.

So you can forget about life insurance, right?

Probably. But there are exceptions.

If you bought a permanent life-insurance policy in your 30s and 40s, you may have good reason to keep it. The accumulated cash value within the policy can offer financial flexibility as you age, a relatively safe and easy way to save and invest money and, in some cases, a competitive interest rate as your funds grow tax-deferred.

Say you don't have a permanent policy. In that case, purchasing life insurance in your 50s and 60s can make sense in certain situations - even if no family members rely on your income.

"Sometimes, a life-insurance need continues beyond age 50 and even into retirement," said Glenn Daily, a fee-only insurance consultant in New York City.

If you live in a state with high estate tax, for example, life-insurance proceeds can help your estate pay those taxes. What's more, life insurance provides liquidity when your assets are illiquid (such as a closely held business or real estate).

Before buying a term policy, ask yourself, 'If I die tomorrow, will my loved ones face any financial problems?'

Term insurance can play a role too. Here's one scenario: You're 60 and have a sibling or other dependent who struggles financially or faces other challenges. Buying a term insurance policy can protect your loved one for 10 or even 20 years if you die during that period.

Before buying a term policy, ask yourself, "If I die tomorrow, will my loved ones face any financial problems?"

"Maybe having that term insurance will let your spouse stay at home or pay for your kid's college [if you die]," said Brian Harrison, president of Savvi Financial, a platform that helps employees and businesses optimize benefits and financial planning.

As long as you're in good health, you can buy term insurance in your 50s and 60s that can bridge the gap until you retire, perhaps covering the last 10 years of paying off your mortgage.

But there's a downside: While term insurance is priced competitively if you're healthy and younger, expect to spend much more (and face medical underwriting) when you're older.

Some term products have a conversion option, meaning they can convert from term - a temporary policy that ends when the term ends - to a permanent policy that stays in force as long as you live and pay the premium. And you won't need to undergo a medical exam to switch.

Before buying a term policy, Harrison suggests asking, "How long within the life of this term can I convert this policy to permanent life insurance?"

"Not all term polices have conversion features," Harrison said. "Often, the conversion feature doesn't extend past 65 or 70 [years of age] and may limit the conversion to the first five or 10 years of the policy. And the older you are, the more expensive it is."

Whole life vs. universal life

If you wind up shopping for a permanent life-insurance policy, an agent may discuss two types: whole life and universal life. Regardless of which type you choose, a sizable chunk of your premium will go toward the agent's commission.

With a whole life-insurance policy, for example, the base commission often exceeds 80% of the premium you pay in the first year. And few agents "will voluntarily reduce their commission to minimum base," Daily said.

If you're considering a whole life policy, Daily suggests asking the agent, "What is the minimum base commission as a percentage of the total death benefit?"

"Your goal is to create a lower-commission, higher-value whole life policy," Daily said. "You can negotiate the commission," and depending on the agent and the insurer issuing the policy, you can save a bundle and start accumulating cash value sooner.

"All five major carriers [Guardian, MassMutual, New York Life, Northwestern Mutual and Penn Mutual] allow varying degrees of commission reduction on whole life policies," Daily added.

If you're weighing any kind of life-insurance purchase, you may want help navigating the complex, confusing set of choices ahead. Agents earn commissions, so they're hardly unbiased. But there are many honest, knowledgeable ones.

So-called captive agents sell policies from just one carrier. They may earn at least a partial salary from that carrier rather than rely solely on commission, and they work for large well-established companies such as USAA, Allstate and State Farm.

Instead, you may want to seek out an independent insurance professional. Independent agents sell policies from different insurers and can shop around on your behalf.

More: Here's what happens to your HSA when you go on Medicare - and how to keep up the tax savings

Also read: 'I feel the clock ticking': My wife and I are in our 60s - and employ 48 people in a small town. Can we ever retire?

-Morey Stettner

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires

data.title: Klarna Deadline: KLAR Investors with Losses in Excess of $100K Have Opportunity to Lead Klarna Group plc Securities Lawsuit First Filed by The Rosen Law Firm
data.description:
Klarna Deadline: KLAR Investors with Losses in Excess of $100K Have Opportunity to Lead Klarna Group plc Securities Lawsuit First Filed by The Rosen Law Firm

Klarna Deadline: KLAR Investors with Losses in Excess of $100K Have Opportunity to Lead Klarna Group plc Securities Lawsuit First Filed by The Rosen Law Firm

PR Newswire

NEW YORK, Feb. 14, 2026

NEW YORK, Feb. 14, 2026 /PRNewswire/ --

Why: Rosen Law Firm, a global investor rights law firm, reminds purchasers of securities of Klarna Group plc (NYSE: KLAR) pursuant and/or traceable to the registration statement and related prospectus (collectively, the "Registration Statement") issued in connection with Klarna's September 2025 initial public offering (the "IPO"), of the important February 20, 2026 lead plaintiff deadline in the securities class action first filed by the Firm.

So What: If you purchased Klarna securities you may be entitled to compensation without payment of any out of pocket fees or costs through a contingency fee arrangement.

What to do next: To join the Klarna class action, go to https://rosenlegal.com/submit-form/?case_id=48971 or call Phillip Kim, Esq. toll-free at 866-767-3653 or email case@rosenlegal.com for information on the class action. A class action lawsuit has already been filed. If you wish to serve as lead plaintiff, you must move the Court no later than February 20, 2026. A lead plaintiff is a representative party acting on behalf of other class members in directing the litigation.

Why Rosen Law: We encourage investors to select qualified counsel with a track record of success in leadership roles. Often, firms issuing notices do not have comparable experience, resources, or any meaningful peer recognition. Many of these firms do not actually litigate securities class actions, but are merely middlemen that refer clients or partner with law firms that actually litigate the cases. Be wise in selecting counsel. The Rosen Law Firm represents investors throughout the globe, concentrating its practice in securities class actions and shareholder derivative litigation. Rosen Law Firm has achieved, at that time, the largest ever securities class action settlement against a Chinese Company. Rosen Law Firm was Ranked No. 1 by ISS Securities Class Action Services for number of securities class action settlements in 2017. The firm has been ranked in the top 4 each year since 2013 and has recovered hundreds of millions of dollars for investors. In 2019 alone the firm secured over $438 million for investors. In 2020, founding partner Laurence Rosen was named by law360 as a Titan of Plaintiffs' Bar. Many of the firm's attorneys have been recognized by Lawdragon and Super Lawyers.

Details of the case: According to the lawsuit, the Registration Statement contained false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (1) Defendants materially understated the risk that Klarna's loss reserves would materially go up within a few months of the IPO, which they either knew of or should have known of given the risk profile of many individuals agreeing to Klarna's buy now, pay later ("BNPL") loans; and (2); as a result, defendants' public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times and negligently prepared. When the true details entered the market, the lawsuit claims that investors suffered damages.  

To join the Klarna class action, go to https://rosenlegal.com/submit-form/?case_id=48971 or call Phillip Kim, Esq. toll-free at 866-767-3653 or email case@rosenlegal.com for information on the class action.

No Class Has Been Certified. Until a class is certified, you are not represented by counsel unless you retain one. You may select counsel of your choice. You may also remain an absent class member and do nothing at this point. An investor's ability to share in any potential future recovery is not dependent upon serving as lead plaintiff.

Follow us for updates on LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/company/the-rosen-law-firm or on Twitter: https://twitter.com/rosen_firm or on Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/rosenlawfirm.

Attorney Advertising. Prior results do not guarantee a similar outcome.

Contact Information:

      Laurence Rosen, Esq.
      Phillip Kim, Esq.
      The Rosen Law Firm, P.A.
      275 Madison Avenue, 40th Floor
      New York, NY  10016
      Tel: (212) 686-1060
      Toll Free: (866) 767-3653
      Fax: (212) 202-3827
      case@rosenlegal.com
      www.rosenlegal.com

data.title: Melissa & Doug Founders Inducted into Toy Hall of Fame as Brand's Commitment to Purposeful Play Takes Center Stage at Toy Fair® 2026
data.description:
Melissa & Doug Founders Inducted into Toy Hall of Fame as Brand's Commitment to Purposeful Play Takes Center Stage at Toy Fair® 2026

Melissa & Doug Founders Inducted into Toy Hall of Fame as Brand's Commitment to Purposeful Play Takes Center Stage at Toy Fair® 2026

PR Newswire

WILTON, Conn., Feb. 14, 2026

WILTON, Conn., Feb. 14, 2026 /PRNewswire/ - Doug and Melissa Bernstein, founders of the purpose-driven children's brand Melissa & Doug®, were inducted into the Toy Industry Hall of Fame at the 2026 Toy Foundation's Toy of the Year® Awards Gala in New York City. The honor recognizes the duo's lasting influence on the toy industry and their decades-long commitment to creating toys that spark imagination and support meaningful child development through purposeful play.

Throughout their careers, Melissa and Doug approached toymaking with a clear and enduring philosophy: take timeless play patterns and elevate and extend them through innovation. By combining a purposeful focus on learning through play, high-quality materials, original artwork, and realistic details, they reimagined classic toys and introduced new ones that invite deeper engagement and open-ended exploration.

Over the course of three decades, that philosophy helped build Melissa & Doug into a beloved global brand and a leader in early developmental play, while achieving #1 Preschool Brand for Wooden & Sustainable Toys and #1 Brand in Children's Puzzles, among many other recognitions.* Signature products such as the Scoop & Serve Ice Cream Counter™, Dust! Sweep! Mop!™, and a robust collection of wooden puzzles—continue to resonate with today's parents and educators, becoming staples in homes and classrooms alike.

"Melissa and Doug are true icons for all of us who are part of the Melissa & Doug family and across our industry," said Lauren DeFeo Duchene, President of Melissa & Doug and EVP at Spin Master. "Their belief in purposeful play—play sparked by a child's imagination and fueled by endless possibilities—established a foundation we're proud to build on today. They created a brand shaped by word-of-mouth love and trust from families, educators, and child-development experts. Their legacy reflects a deep respect for childhood and has made a lasting impact on generations of children."

The Toy Industry Hall of Fame induction coincides with Toy Fair® 2026, where Melissa & Doug is showcasing its latest innovations rooted in that same philosophy of purposeful play. The brand's presence highlights how its open-ended, kid-powered, thoughtfully designed toys can support children from birth to age six at every stage of development.

In addition to the Hall of Fame festivities, Melissa & Doug celebrated nominations for Toy of the Year® for:

  • Melissa & Doug Blockables™ - Construction Toy of the Year
  • Melissa & Doug Simmer & Stir Stovetop Play Set™ - Preschool Toy of the Year

About Melissa & Doug

From timeless wooden and developmental toys to pretend play, puzzles, and arts & crafts, Melissa & Doug products inspire the kind of kid-powered, purposeful play that builds the skills and confidence for children to thrive. Because the way kids play today shapes who they will become tomorrow. Melissa & Doug is a wholly owned subsidiary of Spin Master Corp. (TSX:TOY), a leading global children's entertainment company.  Visit MelissaAndDoug.com for more.

* #1 Preschool Brand for Wooden & Sustainable Toys (Source: Leger 360 Claims Research, March 2025); #1 Brand in Children's Puzzles (Source: Circana LLC, Retail Tracking Service, US Toys, Dollar Sales, Jan – Dec 2025)

SOURCE Melissa & Doug

data.title: 'You're a line item on a spreadsheet.' The case for why you don't need to love your job.
data.description:
By Venessa Wong

Watch out for this invisible financial risk

"I tell people all the time that you can love your job. Just make sure it's not the reason you stop paying attention to what you're worth," a career coach said.

For many American workers, years of labor-market volatility and soaring costs have made them feel like it's impossible to love their jobs.

Three-fourths of workers surveyed by Glassdoor said they do not believe it is possible for people to do what they love in today's job market.

That sentiment was similar across age groups and gender, data shared with MarketWatch show. While results of the December survey, which primarily polled "white-collar" workers, were only broken out for a few industries, tech had the highest share (81%) of people who said it was not possible to do what they love.

This perspective on work, though bleak on one hand, may actually be financially beneficial for workers, career experts told MarketWatch.

Loving your job "costs people real money," said career coach Reno Perry, founder of Career Leap.

"First, there's the unpaid overtime," he told MarketWatch. "Second, and this is the bigger one, loving your job can make you complacent about your market value." Some of his clients haven't interviewed for other jobs in nearly a decade - something he sees as a missed opportunity. "I tell people all the time that you can love your job. Just make sure it's not the reason you stop paying attention to what you're worth."

Complacent high earners may be making tens of thousands of dollars less each year than they could be making elsewhere, Perry said, which has a significant "compounding effect on retirement savings, equity and future negotiating leverage."

More on MarketWatch: Americans' happiness is at a record low. Are we just using our money the wrong way?

The concept that workers should love their job emerged as the U.S. shifted from having a largely industrial workforce to a heavily service- and knowledge-based workforce, Sarah Jaffe, author of "Work Won't Love You Back," told MarketWatch.

"You didn't expect anybody to love standing on the automobile assembly line," she said. But there was a notion that people could harbor a passion for other types of work, she added - and companies could exploit that passion.

The tech industry then popularized "cool" office perks like free meals and gyms that made them attractive workplaces but often kept people working longer hours, Jaffe said, or made employees more dependent on their employers for nonwork-related engagements like exercise or socializing.

Several factors disrupted people's relationships with work in recent years. Rapidly rising costs outpaced most people's income growth; the pandemic exposed how vulnerable workers were to economic shocks; and now, AI is threatening to replace jobs.

Job security was a top concern for workers heading into 2026, and recent surveys have shown that even people making $100,000 are now worried about the economy and the labor market. Though the overall U.S. economy is humming along, hiring is weak and increasingly concentrated in just a couple of sectors: healthcare and hospitality.

Related: Layoff anxiety is soaring. Here's how you can 'prepare rather than panic.'

Unsettled workers are realizing that what they want isn't to love their job, but "a decent paycheck and some stability," Jaffe said. In fact, 93% of workers told Glassdoor last month that they've stayed in a job they didn't love because of the stability it provided.

That doesn't mean company culture isn't important. Most workers still want their jobs to be "about more than financial success or just a necessary evil to pay the rent," author and Harvard professor Arthur Brooks wrote last year in the Atlantic.

Managers should understand that people still benefit when they feel their efforts lead to value, which he calls "earned success," and when they feel needed or that their work is making life better for someone else, Brooks said in a social-media post.

So-called job huggers, people who stay in their jobs, can also try to focus on projects that energize them, or ask to switch teams or managers to make their workdays better, according to Glassdoor's report.

Even for employees who enjoy work, letting go of the illusion that their job can love them back can provide "clarity," Career Leap's Perry said. "You start making career decisions based on strategy instead of emotion."

Workers should keep their skills up to date, their network active and their options open. "You can give your best work, be fully engaged and still recognize that if the company's priorities shift, you're a line item on a spreadsheet," he added.

JOIN THE DON'T SHORT YOURSELF LIVE Q&ATax season stressing you out? We've got you. Join Don't Short Yourself Live to ask MarketWatch's Andrew Keshner and Beth Pinsker your tax questions on Wednesday, Feb. 25, 2026, at 1:30 p.m. Eastern time.

What personal-finance issues would you like to see covered in MarketWatch? We would like to hear from readers about their financial decisions and money-related questions. You can write to us at readerstories@marketwatch.com. A reporter may be in touch to learn more. MarketWatch will not attribute your answers to you by name without your permission.

-Venessa Wong

  (END) Dow Jones Newswires

data.title: Big Warner Bros. shareholders are losing patience with the Paramount-Netflix bidding war
data.description:
By Kenneth Rapoza

Institutional owners of Warner Bros. shares might jump ship as Paramount ups the ante

Many investors care only about the spread between the bid and final price. They also care about regulatory risk. They don't want long court battles.

With a shareholder vote likely on the horizon, Paramount Skydance (PSKY) is unrelenting in its hostile tender offer to woo Warner Bros Discovery (WBD) (WBD) investors away from rival bidder Netflix (NFLX).

The question now is how impatient large WBD investors will become with the board, especially considering Paramount offered them more for the WBD shares they recently purchased after Netflix announced its bid.

In normal times, a corporate board focuses on long-term strategy - improving a company's chances of surviving and competing over many years. Courts presume that this forward-looking stewardship ultimately benefits investors.

But the calculus changes once a company is put up for sale. At that point, the board's legal duty narrows, and its main obligation becomes maximizing shareholder value. Paramount's hostile takeover bypasses management entirely, appealing directly to investors and potentially putting shareholders' preferences at odds with the board's preferred transaction.

Since Netflix's bid in early December, several investment funds have disclosed increased exposure to WBD's stock, often as part of merger-arbitrage strategies betting on the deal's completion or further bidding. The investor data are still rolling out, with 13F filings for the fourth quarter of 2025 not due until Feb. 14. But clearly, many investors went long WBD in December in anticipation of a sale. Will enough of them accept the $30-per-share offer later this month and give Paramount a controlling stake in WBD? It could happen.

The BlackRock Event Driven Equity Fund BILPX, for example, has increased its WBD holdings by 374% as of Dec. 31, while the Vanguard Windsor II Fund VWNFX increased its holdings by 15%, according to investment researcher Morningstar data.

Oakmark Funds, which owns around 3.8% of Warner Bros.' outstanding shares according to Morningstar, said in a Dec. 31 note to clients: "We are pleased with the steps the WBD board has taken thus far to unlock shareholder value. We will continue to closely monitor developments as this bidding war unfolds."

On Jan. 20, investor David Einhorn wrote in a letter to his firm's investors that his Greenlight Capital bought WBD because of the dueling offers. They're waiting for a deal to settle, with an expected final share price "in the low to mid $30s," Einhorn wrote. That's the Paramount number.

Both Oakmark and Greenlight sound like sellers. This type of investor cares about the spread between the bid and final price. They also care about regulatory risk. They don't want long court battles.

The New York Post's Charlie Gasparino reported earlier this month that Warner Bros. CEO David Zaslav wants Paramount to sweeten its $30-a-share bid. To get them to do that, he filed paperwork for an expedited "proxy" vote to approve Netflix's $72 billion offer. Paramount is unlikely to increase its number. Gasparino's take is that filing for an expedited proxy vote is about pressuring Paramount and its deal partners at RedBird Capital to provide more financial guarantees.

If enough shareholders tender at $30, Paramount could gain a larger stake in WBD and force the board into a fiduciary dilemma.

Deal offers have changed for both sides.

Netflix pivoted late last month to match Paramount's all-cash deal worth $27.75 per WBD share for the streaming and studios assets. WBD's board favors this proposal.

The fact that Paramount's offer is for the entire company, meanwhile, signals that their deal might lead to a quicker closing for investors. Paramount this week extended the deadline of its tender offer to March 2. If enough shareholders tender at $30, Paramount could gain a larger stake in WBD and force the board into a fiduciary dilemma.

Some investors are taking Paramount's offer, though the company is still far short of the 50% shares needed to gain effective control and force the WBD board to sell to Paramount. It is still early in the game, and early tenders in hostile bids are often small.

Since a number of these investors have bought in the fourth quarter because of bid rumors and Paramount's higher dollar offer, they are into this stock for fast closure.

Paramount can position its bid to shareholders as the "competitive counterweight" to both political risk and legal risk.

For its part, Washington keeps signaling that Netflix faces real regulatory risk. Netflix Chief Ted Sarandos and Warner Bros. Chief Revenue and Strategy Officer Bruce Campbell testified during a Feb. 3 Senate Judiciary Committee hearing about the deal.

Committee Chairman Mike Lee, a Republican from Utah, is already on record saying the deal "raised serious antitrust concerns." Ranking Member Cory Booker, a Democrat from New Jersey, said in his opening remarks, "I believe that we are now seeing consolidation that is screwing Americans."

Meanwhile, in the U.K., politicians urged the Competition and Markets Authority to review Netflix's bid, saying it would reinforce Netflix's dominance in the U.K. market. Members of the European Parliament and cinema associations are lobbying the European Commission to examine the deal.

Paramount can position its bid to shareholders as the "competitive counterweight" to both political risk and legal risk. If U.S. regulators really don't want the country's biggest filmed content streamer to own HBO and Warner's studios, then they'll ax the Netflix deal. Shareholders who tendered will join a class-action lawsuit against Netflix if their offer wins, leading to market uncertainty.

While Paramount buys time, the Netflix offer is moving further toward antitrust scrutiny. Any regulatory headlines will widen the risk gap between these two deals - giving Paramount the edge if investors compare that risk side-by-side.

Kenneth Rapoza is an analyst for the Coalition for a Prosperous America, which represents U.S. producers and workers. Rapoza has no positions in any of the stocks mentioned.
